question:
B-1. Which configurations of five (not necessarily distinct) points \( p_{1}, \cdots, p_{\text {s }} \) on the circle \( x^{2}+y^{2}=1 \) maximize the sum of the ten distances
\[
\sum_{i<1} d\left(p_{i}, p_{i}\right) ?
\]
[Here \( d(p, q) \) denotes the straight line distance between \( p \) and \( q \).]
solution:
B-1.
Since the \( p_{i} \) need not be distinct, the sum is a continuous function on the compact set \( C \times C \times C \times C \times C \), where \( C \) is the circle. Hence maxima exist. One proves that the maximum occurs when the \( p_{i} \) are the vertices of a regular pentagon by showing that this configuration simultaneously maximizes both of the sums:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
S=d\left(p_{1}, p_{2}\right)+d\left(p_{2}, p_{3}\right)+d\left(p_{3}, p_{4}\right)+d\left(p_{4}, p_{5}\right)+d\left(p_{5}, p_{1}\right), \\
T=d\left(p_{1}, p_{3}\right)+d\left(p_{2}, p_{4}\right)+d\left(p_{3}, p_{5}\right)+d\left(p_{4}, p_{1}\right)+d\left(p_{5}, p_{2}\right) .
\end{array}
\]

For \( S \) or \( T \), one can fix four of the points; then the varying part of the sum is of the form.
\[
D=d(p, a)+d(p, b), \text { with } a \text { and } b \text { fixed. }
\]

Using the Law of Sines, one shows that \( D \) is a constant times \( \sin \alpha+\sin \beta \) where \( \alpha=\Varangle p a b \), \( \beta=\Varangle p b a \), and \( \alpha+\beta \) is constant. Then it is easy to show that \( D \) is not a maximum unless \( p \) is symmetrically situated with respect to \( a \) and \( b \).

descriptive_long.question:
B-1. Which configurations of five (not necessarily distinct) points \( pointone, \cdots, pointgen_{\text{indexs}} \) on the circle \( xcoord^{2}+ycoord^{2}=1 \) maximize the sum of the ten distances
\[
\sum_{indexi<1} d\left(pointi, pointi\right) ?
\]
[Here \( d(pointgen, pointque) \) denotes the straight line distance between \( pointgen \) and \( pointque \).]
descriptive_long.solution:
B-1.
Since the \( pointi \) need not be distinct, the sum is a continuous function on the compact set \( C \times C \times C \times C \times C \), where \( C \) is the circle. Hence maxima exist. One proves that the maximum occurs when the \( pointi \) are the vertices of a regular pentagon by showing that this configuration simultaneously maximizes both of the sums:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
sumsucc=d\left(pointone, pointtwo\right)+d\left(pointtwo, pointthr\right)+d\left(pointthr, pointfor\right)+d\left(pointfor, pointfiv\right)+d\left(pointfiv, pointone\right), \\
sumother=d\left(pointone, pointthr\right)+d\left(pointtwo, pointfor\right)+d\left(pointthr, pointfiv\right)+d\left(pointfor, pointone\right)+d\left(pointfiv, pointtwo\right) .
\end{array}
\]

For \( sumsucc \) or \( sumother \), one can fix four of the points; then the varying part of the sum is of the form.
\[
distvar=d(pointgen, parpointa)+d(pointgen, parpointb), \text { with } parpointa \text { and } parpointb \text { fixed. }
\]

Using the Law of Sines, one shows that \( distvar \) is a constant times \( \sin angleone+\sin angletwo \) where \( angleone=\Varangle pointgen\ parpointa\ parpointb \), \( angletwo=\Varangle pointgen\ parpointb\ parpointa \), and \( angleone+angletwo \) is constant. Then it is easy to show that \( distvar \) is not a maximum unless \( pointgen \) is symmetrically situated with respect to \( parpointa \) and \( parpointb \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
B-1. Which configurations of five (not necessarily distinct) points \( whistleray, \cdots, glimmerfog_{\text {s }} \) on the circle \( tulipseed^{2}+cobaltash^{2}=1 \) maximize the sum of the ten distances
\[
\sum_{orchardmist<1} d\left(glimmerfog_{orchardmist}, glimmerfog_{orchardmist}\right) ?
\]
[Here \( d(glimmerfog, zephyrnote) \) denotes the straight line distance between \( glimmerfog \) and \( zephyrnote \).]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
B-1.
Since the \( glimmerfog_{orchardmist} \) need not be distinct, the sum is a continuous function on the compact set \( C \times C \times C \times C \times C \), where \( C \) is the circle. Hence maxima exist. One proves that the maximum occurs when the \( glimmerfog_{orchardmist} \) are the vertices of a regular pentagon by showing that this configuration simultaneously maximizes both of the sums:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
frostedvale=d\left(glimmerfog_{1}, glimmerfog_{2}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{2}, glimmerfog_{3}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{3}, glimmerfog_{4}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{4}, glimmerfog_{5}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{5}, glimmerfog_{1}\right), \\
crimsonhaze=d\left(glimmerfog_{1}, glimmerfog_{3}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{2}, glimmerfog_{4}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{3}, glimmerfog_{5}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{4}, glimmerfog_{1}\right)+d\left(glimmerfog_{5}, glimmerfog_{2}\right) .
\end{array}
\]

For \( frostedvale \) or \( crimsonhaze \), one can fix four of the points; then the varying part of the sum is of the form.
\[
meadowglen=d(glimmerfog, thundersail)+d(glimmerfog, velvetpeak), \text { with } thundersail \text { and } velvetpeak \text { fixed. }
\]

Using the Law of Sines, one shows that \( meadowglen \) is a constant times \( \sin sunlitcove+\sin rainwooden \) where \( sunlitcove=\Varangle glimmerfog\, thundersail\, velvetpeak \), \( rainwooden=\Varangle glimmerfog\, velvetpeak\, thundersail \), and \( sunlitcove+rainwooden \) is constant. Then it is easy to show that \( meadowglen \) is not a maximum unless \( glimmerfog \) is symmetrically situated with respect to \( thundersail \) and \( velvetpeak \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
B-1. Which configurations of five (not necessarily distinct) points \( nullpointone, \cdots, voidentity_{\text {infinitesize }} \) on the circle \( verticalcoor^{2}+horizontalcoor^{2}=1 \) maximize the sum of the ten distances
\[
\sum_{continuousvar<1} d\left(nullcollection, nullcollection\right) ?
\]
[Here \( d(voidentity, constantval) \) denotes the straight line distance between \( voidentity \) and \( constantval \).]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
B-1.
Since the \( voidentity_{continuousvar} \) need not be distinct, the sum is a continuous function on the compact set \( C \times C \times C \times C \times C \), where \( C \) is the circle. Hence maxima exist. One proves that the maximum occurs when the \( voidentity_{continuousvar} \) are the vertices of a regular pentagon by showing that this configuration simultaneously maximizes both of the sums:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
differenceval=d\left(nullpointone, nullpointtwo\right)+d\left(nullpointtwo, nullpointthr\right)+d\left(nullpointthr, nullpointfou\right)+d\left(nullpointfou, nullpointfiv\right)+d\left(nullpointfiv, nullpointone\right), \\
productval=d\left(nullpointone, nullpointthr\right)+d\left(nullpointtwo, nullpointfou\right)+d\left(nullpointthr, nullpointfiv\right)+d\left(nullpointfou, nullpointone\right)+d\left(nullpointfiv, nullpointtwo\right) .
\end{array}
\]

For \( differenceval \) or \( productval \), one can fix four of the points; then the varying part of the sum is of the form.
\[
proximity=d(voidentity, endpoint)+d(voidentity, midpoint), \text { with } endpoint \text { and } midpoint \text { fixed. }
\]

Using the Law of Sines, one shows that \( proximity \) is a constant times \( \sin straightness+\sin flatness \) where \( straightness=\Varangle voidentity endpoint midpoint \), \( flatness=\Varangle voidentity midpoint endpoint \), and \( straightness+flatness \) is constant. Then it is easy to show that \( proximity \) is not a maximum unless \( voidentity \) is symmetrically situated with respect to \( endpoint \) and \( midpoint \).

garbled_string.question:
B-1. Which configurations of five (not necessarily distinct) points \(afyerhqd, \cdots, kwzdphqa_{\text {fqeodkri }}\) on the circle \(lqmrznxh^{2}+vgtsckbl^{2}=1\) maximize the sum of the ten distances
\[
\sum_{rupdxeon<1} d\left(qnjtmsvo, qnjtmsvo\right) ?
\]
[Here \( d(kwzdphqa, tbkglsme) \) denotes the straight line distance between \( kwzdphqa \) and \( tbkglsme \).]
garbled_string.solution:
Since the \( qnjtmsvo \) need not be distinct, the sum is a continuous function on the compact set \( C \times C \times C \times C \times C \), where \( C \) is the circle. Hence maxima exist. One proves that the maximum occurs when the \( qnjtmsvo \) are the vertices of a regular pentagon by showing that this configuration simultaneously maximizes both of the sums:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
bermwqlj=d\left(afyerhqd, ghzlptwa\right)+d\left(ghzlptwa, jcvmorbe\right)+d\left(jcvmorbe, mydkseuh\right)+d\left(mydkseuh, ozwcrnfa\right)+d\left(ozwcrnfa, afyerhqd\right), \\
szxavcno=d\left(afyerhqd, jcvmorbe\right)+d\left(ghzlptwa, mydkseuh\right)+d\left(jcvmorbe, ozwcrnfa\right)+d\left(mydkseuh, afyerhqd\right)+d\left(ozwcrnfa, ghzlptwa\right) .
\end{array}
\]

For \( bermwqlj \) or \( szxavcno \), one can fix four of the points; then the varying part of the sum is of the form.
\[
uwrqgpyh=d(kwzdphqa, mtylgzsd)+d(kwzdphqa, xprfucen), \text { with } mtylgzsd \text { and } xprfucen \text { fixed. }
\]

Using the Law of Sines, one shows that \( uwrqgpyh \) is a constant times \( \sin qzxwvtnp+\sin hjgrksla \) where \( qzxwvtnp=\Varangle kwzdphqa mtylgzsd xprfucen \), \( hjgrksla=\Varangle kwzdphqa xprfucen mtylgzsd \), and \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla \) is constant. Then it is easy to show that \( uwrqgpyh \) is not a maximum unless \( kwzdphqa \) is symmetrically situated with respect to \( mtylgzsd \) and \( xprfucen \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let C be the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4.  For seven distinct points P_1, P_2, \ldots  , P_7 on C put
        \Sigma   =  \sum _{1 \leq  i < j \leq  7} d(P_i , P_j),
the sum of their \(\binom{7}{2}=21\) pairwise Euclidean distances.
Determine   
(a) the maximal possible value of \Sigma , and  
(b) all configurations of the seven points that realise this maximum.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write R = 2 and identify the circle C with the complex numbers of modulus R.

Step 1 - An angular description.
After a rigid rotation we may assume
        0 = \theta _1 < \theta _2 < \cdots  < \theta _7 < 2\pi .                    (1)
Put P_k = R e^{i\theta _k}.  Introduce the successive gaps
        \alpha _k = \theta _{k+1} - \theta _k   (k = 1,\ldots ,7 , \theta _8 := \theta _1+2\pi )            (2)
so that
        \alpha _1+\cdots +\alpha _7 = 2\pi  , \alpha _k > 0 .                               (3)

For two points whose (smaller) central angle is \varphi  \in  (0,2\pi ) the chord length equals
        d = 2R sin(\varphi /2) = 4 sin(\varphi /2).                          (4)

For the seven points every unordered pair is characterised by its cyclic index distance k = 1,2,3.  (Distance 4 gives the same chord as 3 because `going the other way round' yields the complementary angle 2\pi -\varphi , but (4) shows the chord is the same.)  Set
        \beta _{i,k} = \alpha _i + \alpha _{i+1} + \ldots  + \alpha _{i+k-1}   (indices mod 7).  (5)
With f(t) :=  sin(t/2) we get
        \Sigma (\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _7) = 4  \sum _{k=1}^{3}  \sum _{i=1}^{7}  f(\beta _{i,k}).        (6)
The right-hand side contains exactly the 21 distances.

Step 2 - A Jensen-type upper bound.
The function f(t)=sin(t/2) is concave on (0,2\pi ) because f''(t)=-\frac{1}{4} sin(t/2)<0.  For every fixed k\in {1,2,3} we apply Jensen's inequality to the seven numbers \beta _{1,k},\ldots ,\beta _{7,k} :
        (1/7)\sum _{i=1}^{7} f(\beta _{i,k}) \leq  f\bigl((1/7)\sum _{i=1}^{7} \beta _{i,k}\bigr).     (7)
Equality occurs iff \beta _{1,k}=\cdots =\beta _{7,k}.

Because each \alpha _j occurs exactly k times in the sum \sum _{i} \beta _{i,k},
        \sum _{i=1}^{7} \beta _{i,k} = k(\alpha _1+\cdots +\alpha _7)=k\cdot 2\pi ,                 (8)
so the average in (7) equals (k\cdot 2\pi )/7.  Insert this into (7) and multiply by 7:
        \sum _{i=1}^{7} f(\beta _{i,k}) \leq  7 f(k\cdot 2\pi /7).                     (9)
Using (6) we obtain the global inequality
        \Sigma (\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _7) \leq  4\cdot 7\cdot [ f(2\pi /7)+f(4\pi /7)+f(6\pi /7) ]              (10)
        = 4\cdot 7\cdot [ sin(\pi /7)+sin(2\pi /7)+sin(3\pi /7) ].

Step 3 - Characterisation of the equality case.
For k = 1, inequality (9) reads \sum  f(\alpha _i) \leq  7 f(2\pi /7).  Equality in (10) therefore forces equality in the k=1 instance of (9), whence
        \alpha _1 = \alpha _2 = \cdots  = \alpha _7 = 2\pi /7.                               (11)
Conversely, the equal-gap point (11) indeed realises equality in (9) for k=1,2,3, hence attains the upper bound (10).

Thus the only maximising 7-tuple (\alpha _1,\ldots ,\alpha _7) is (2\pi /7,\ldots ,2\pi /7).  Up to a cyclic shift - which merely renames the points - there is no other possibility.

Step 4 - Evaluation of the maximum.
Insert (11) into (6).  Now \beta _{i,1}=2\pi /7, \beta _{i,2}=4\pi /7 and \beta _{i,3}=6\pi /7, so
  \Sigma _max = 4 \cdot  7 \cdot  ( sin(\pi /7) + sin(2\pi /7) + sin(3\pi /7) )
         \approx  4 \cdot  7 \cdot  2.1906419\ldots 
         \approx  61.34 .                                            (12)
(Using sin(\pi /7)\approx 0.433883, sin(2\pi /7)\approx 0.781831, sin(3\pi /7)\approx 0.974928.)

Step 5 - Geometric description of the maximisers.
Because \Sigma  is invariant under rigid motions of C, rotating the unique angular configuration (11) around the centre produces every maximising 7-tuple (P_1,\ldots ,P_7).  In other words, the points must be the seven vertices of some regular heptagon inscribed in x^2+y^2 = 4, and every such heptagon indeed gives the maximal value (12).

Answer.
(a) The maximal possible value of \Sigma  is
        4\cdot 7\cdot [ sin(\pi /7)+sin(2\pi /7)+sin(3\pi /7) ] \approx  61.34.
(b) It is attained exactly when the seven points are the vertices of a regular heptagon on the circle x^2 + y^2 = 4 (all such heptagons being congruent by rotation).

core_steps: Use compactness of C^5 to guarantee a maximizing configuration exists.
Write the total pair-sum as S (distances of neighbors) + T (distances of next-neighbors).
With four points fixed, the part that varies is D = d(p,a)+d(p,b).
Apply the Law of Sines to get D = k( sinα + sinβ ) with α+β fixed; this is maximized when α = β, i.e. p is symmetric w.r.t. a and b.
Iterating that symmetry for every vertex yields equally spaced vertices ⇒ regular pentagon.

slot1.description: Radius of the circle; any rescaling multiplies every distance by the same constant and does not affect which configuration maximizes the sum.

slot2.description: Cardinality of the point set; the same symmetry argument works for n points and would lead to a regular n-gon.

slot3.description: Permission for coincident points; forbidding or allowing repeats does not change the maximizer because the optimum has distinct points anyway.
